verses.commentary: On a Sabbath — disciples picking heads of grain, rubbing in hands, eating. Permitted by Torah (Deuteronomy 23:25); counted as reaping by Pharisaic tradition.
Pharisees: 'Why do you do what is not lawful on the Sabbath?' Tradition projected as Torah.
Jesus: 'Have you not read what David did when he was hungry?' Familiar precedent — they have read it but missed the point.
David entered the house of God, took and ate the bread of the Presence (1 Samuel 21), which was not lawful except for priests, and gave to those with him. Royal hunger overrides ritual restriction.
'The Son of Man is Lord of the Sabbath.' Christological supremacy over the institution. The Sabbath is for him to rule, not for him to be ruled by.
Another Sabbath — synagogue teaching. Man with a withered right hand. Detail of 'right hand' is Lukan precision (Mark has 'a hand').
Scribes and Pharisees watch closely to see if Jesus would heal on the Sabbath — to accuse him. The suffering man is a trap.
Jesus knows their thoughts; tells the man: 'Rise and stand in the middle.' He rises and stands. Public visibility chosen.
'I ask you: is it lawful on the Sabbath to do good or to do harm, to save life or to destroy it?' Forces them onto the ethical question. Doing nothing is not neutral.
Looks around at them all, says: 'Stretch out your hand.' He did, and his hand was restored. No work performed by Jesus — the man stretches and is healed. Trap evaded.
They were filled with fury (anoia, ἀνοία — mindless rage), discuss what to do to Jesus. Conspiracy forms over a healed hand.

Jesus went out to the mountain to pray. Continued in prayer to God all night (dianyktereuon en te proseuche tou theou, διανυκτερεύων ἐν τῇ προσευχῇ τοῦ θεοῦ). Lukan distinctive — major decisions preceded by extended prayer.
Day breaks. Calls disciples, chooses twelve, names them apostles (apostolous, ἀποστόλους — sent ones). Strategic selection after vigil.
Simon (named Peter), Andrew, James, John, Philip, Bartholomew.
Matthew, Thomas, James son of Alphaeus, Simon called the Zealot.
Judas son of James, and Judas Iscariot who became a traitor (egeneto prodotes, ἐγένετο προδότης). Listed with the betrayal-fate attached.
Down with them, stands on a level place (epi topou pedinou, ἐπὶ τόπου πεδινοῦ) — hence 'Sermon on the Plain.' Large crowd from Judea, Jerusalem, Tyre, Sidon (Gentile area noted).
Came to hear and be healed. Those troubled by unclean spirits — cured. The hearing-and-healing crowd setting.
All the crowd sought to touch him; power came out from him; healed them all. Touch-healing en masse.

Looks at his disciples: 'Blessed are you who are poor (ptochoi, πτωχοί), for yours is the kingdom of God.' Lukan beatitudes are sharper than Matthew's — 'poor,' not 'poor in spirit.' Economic reality emphasized.
'Blessed are you who hunger now, for you will be satisfied. Blessed are you who weep now, for you will laugh.' Now-future reversal. Hunger/satiety, weeping/laughter — temporal contrasts.
'Blessed are you when men hate you, exclude you, revile you, and cast out your name as evil, on account of the Son of Man.' Persecution as confirmation of allegiance to the Son of Man.
Rejoice in that day and leap (skirtesate, σκιρτήσατε — same word for John in the womb 1:41) — great is your reward in heaven. Their fathers did the same to the prophets.
'But woe to you who are rich (plousiois, πλουσίοις), for you have received your consolation (paraklesin, παράκλησιν).' Wealth as already-received-comfort. Reversal beatitudes get woes-paired.
'Woe to you who are full now, for you shall be hungry. Woe to you who laugh now, for you shall mourn and weep.' Symmetric inversions of 6:21.
'Woe to you when all men speak well of you — for so their fathers did to the false prophets.' Universal approval as warning. False prophets win social acceptance.
'But I say to you who hear: Love your enemies, do good to those who hate you.' Direct command begins the love-of-enemies section.
Bless those who curse you, pray for those who abuse you. Four enemy-actions, four love-responses.
Cheek slap — turn the other. Cloak taken — give tunic too. Echo of Matthew 5:39-40.
Give to everyone who begs. Do not demand back what is taken. Generosity without retrieval.
Golden Rule: 'Do to others as you would have them do to you.' Lukan version (compare Matthew 7:12).
If you love those who love you, what credit (charis, χάρις — grace, in this sense, recognition) is that? Even sinners do that. Reciprocal love is the floor.
If you do good to those who do good to you. Same logic with 'do good' substituted.
If you lend to those from whom you expect repayment — even sinners lend to sinners to receive back equal amounts. Lending logic should exceed sinners' pattern.

Love enemies, do good, lend expecting nothing in return. Your reward will be great; you will be sons of the Most High; he is kind to the ungrateful and evil. Imitation of God's character defines kinship.
'Be merciful (oiktirmones, οἰκτίρμονες), even as your Father is merciful.' Lukan version of Matthew 5:48 ('perfect') — Luke's mercy-language emphasized.
Judge not — be not judged. Condemn not — be not condemned. Forgive — be forgiven. Triadic reciprocity.
Give — be given. A good measure, pressed down, shaken together, running over, put into your lap. With the measure you use, it will be measured back. Generosity boomerangs.
Parable: can the blind lead the blind? Both fall in a pit. Leadership without sight is corporate calamity.
Disciple not above teacher — when fully trained, will be like his teacher. Discipleship as imitation, not improvement.
Why see the speck in your brother's eye, not notice the log in your own? Echo of Matthew 7:3. The hypocrite-syndrome.
How can you say, 'Brother, let me take out the speck' when you do not see the log? Hypocrite — first take the log out, then you will see clearly.
No good tree bears bad fruit; no bad tree bears good fruit. Nature determines product.
Each tree known by its fruit. People do not gather figs from thorns, grapes from briars. Sources determine harvest.
Good man — out of his good treasure brings forth good; evil man — evil from his evil treasure. The mouth speaks from the heart's overflow. Diagnostic.
'Why do you call me Lord, Lord, and not do what I say?' Confession without obedience is contradiction.
Everyone who comes, hears my words, does them — I will show you what he is like.
Builder who dug deep, laid foundation on rock. Flood comes, river breaks, cannot shake — well built. Lukan specifics: 'dug deep' emphasizes labor.
Who hears, does not do — like a man who built without foundation. River breaks, immediately falls; the ruin is great. The two-builder parable concludes the Plain Sermon.
